title: CXR — Approach
oneLiner: A systematic method to interpret chest X-ray, prioritising life-threatening findings in acutely unwell patients.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax:** Complete white-out of hemithorax with contralateral mediastinal shift (trachea deviated away, heart borders pushed, >50% lung volume). Immediate needle decompression – do not wait for CT.
- **Massive haemothorax / large pleural effusion:** Complete or near-complete opacification of one side with mediastinal shift *away* from the opacification (differentiates from collapse). Respiratory distress + hypotension. Call chest drain team now – not after full workup.
- **Advanced bilateral pneumonia / ARDS:** Diffuse bilateral airspace opacification (white lung) with normal heart size + peripheral sparing? OR bilateral symmetrical opacities + rapidly worsening hypoxia. High PEEP, senior review, ICU referral.
- **Massive pulmonary embolism (saddle embolus):** Normal CXR but undetectable cardiac output + severe hypoxia. **Single finding:** CXR clear + clinical shock – must get CT pulmonary angiogram immediately if stable; if unstable, go straight to echocardiogram and thrombolysis.
sections.Presentation:
- **Indications:** Dyspnoea, chest pain, cough, haemoptysis, fever, trauma, suspected pneumothorax, pleural effusion, lung cancer, pre-operative, post-line/tube check.
- **Key exam findings before CXR:** Wheeze, crackles, decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion, tracheal deviation, JVP elevation, unilateral chest expansion, hypoxia (SpO2 <92% room air).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **For opacification/consolidation:**
  - Pneumonia (often lobar, air bronchogram)
  - Atelectasis (volume loss, fissure shift)
  - Pulmonary oedema (bilateral, perihilar, cardiomegaly)
  - Haemorrhage (bilateral or focal, sudden onset)
- **For hyperlucency:**
  - Simple pneumothorax (pleural line, absent lung markings)
  - COPD/emphysema (flattened diaphragms, hyperinflation)
  - Compensatory hyperinflation after lobectomy
- **Splitter:** **Tracheal deviation + mediastinal shift** → differentiates tension (shift *away* from opacification) from massive collapse (shift *toward* opacification).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Ensure correct patient + side markers (right vs left, AP vs PA, erect vs supine).
  - **Systematic search:** ABCDE approach – Airway (trachea, carina), Breathing (lungs, pleural spaces), Cardiac (heart size, borders, mediastinal width), Diaphragm (costophrenic angles, free air under diaphragm), Everything else (bones, tubes, lines, soft tissues, gastric bubble, apical lung).
  - **Check for lines:** ET tube tip 3-5 cm above carina, CVC tip in SVC, NG tube in stomach, chest tube directed apically/posteriorly.
- **Bloods:**
  - Not directly part of CXR interpretation, but for context: FBC, CRP, U+E, blood cultures if fever, troponin if chest pain, ABG if hypoxia/respiratory failure.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** PA erect CXR (AP supine only if patient cannot sit up).
  - **Second-line:** Lateral CXR (localised opacities, effusion meniscus), CT chest (if complex effusion, suspected PE, lung cancer staging, trauma).
  - **Never-miss test:** **For unexplained hypoxia with clear CXR → CT pulmonary angiogram** (rule out PE before attributing to other causes). Also, **right-sided ECG leads** if suspecting right ventricular strain (S1Q3T3, RVH).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - *Pneumothorax size:* >2 cm measured from lung margin to chest wall at level of hilum (BTS guideline). Supine CXR – air in anterior costophrenic sulcus (deep sulcus sign).
  - *Cardiomegaly:* cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on PA film.
  - *Pleural effusion:* meniscus sign, blunting of costophrenic angle; small <400 mL.
  - *Pneumonia:* CURB-65 score (confusion, urea>7, RR≥30, BP<90/60, age≥65) used to guide admission.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Tension pneumothorax:** Needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line) → chest drain (5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line). Do not wait for CT.
  - **Massive effusion/haemothorax:** Insert large-bore chest drain (28-32Fr) – if haemothorax, consider auto-transfusion.
  - **Massive haemoptysis (opacified lung + hypovolaemia):** Lie patient on affected side to protect healthy lung, call anaesthetics/ICU for rigid bronchoscopy. Do not put in lateral position – they may choke.
  - **Suspected PE with high-probability CXR (normal):** Activate PE protocol – urgent CTPA if stable, echo if unstable.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Validate CXR findings with clinical picture. Document systematic report (use a template).
  - Repeat CXR for: new symptoms, pneumothorax after chest tube management, pneumonia not resolving after 72h, pleural effusion post-drainage.
  - Ensure oxygen therapy based on SpO2 targets (88-92% if risk of CO2 retention, else ≥94%).
  - VTE prophylaxis if prolonged immobility (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD or tinzaparin 3500 IU SC OD).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Abnormal CXR (e.g. nodule, mass, incidental finding) – refer to respiratory clinic or lung cancer MDT per local protocol.
  - Follow up resolution: e.g. repeat CXR at 4-6 weeks for pneumonia if age>50 or smoker.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 1L 0.9% NaCl over 8h if nil by mouth or vomiting – otherwise encourage oral hydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Yes, unless contraindicated (check renal function, weight, platelet count).
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM only if awaiting procedure (e.g. CT with contrast, chest drain insertion). Clear fluids ok.
  - **Observation frequency:** Baseline NEWS2; if stable on air → 4-hourly; if hypoxic/respiratory distress → 1-2 hourly until stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **No first-line drugs specific to CXR interpretation.** However, if CXR shows pneumonia, treat empirically:
  - **Community-acquired pneumonia (CURB-65 ≥2):** Doxycycline 200 mg PO/IV stat, then 100 mg OD x 7 days (if mild/moderate). Alternative: Co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS x 7 days.
  - **Hospital-acquired pneumonia (≥48h in hospital):** Seek microbiology advice; typical: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV QDS x 7 days.
  - **Acute exacerbation COPD (pneumonia excluded):** Prednisolone 30 mg PO OD x 5 days + +/- amoxicillin 500 mg PO TDS x 5 days (only if purulent sputum).
- **Key contraindications:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: allergy, avoid in penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity).
  - Doxycycline: pregnancy/lactation (relative), children <12 (tooth discolouration).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing high-concentration oxygen (e.g. 15 L/min via non-rebreather) in known COPD without target saturation monitoring (risks hypercapnic respiratory failure).** Titrate to SpO2 88-92%.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Misinterpretation leading to delayed treatment (e.g., missed tension pneumothorax, incorrectly diagnosing pneumonia in pulmonary oedema).
  - Failure to detect pneumothorax on supine CXR (deep sulcus sign or subtle hyperlucency).
  - Missed congenital or iatrogenic aortopulmonary window (mediastinal widening – could be aortic dissection).
- **Chronic:**
  - Over-investigation from incidental benign nodule – unnecessary radiation from follow-up CTs.
  - Underdiagnosis of lung cancer due to inadequate technique (poor inspiration, rotated film).
sections.Prognosis: - 1-3 month mortality for community-acquired pneumonia is ~10-20% in hospitalised patients; uncomplicated pneumothorax resolves in days with intervention; pleural effusion outcome depends on aetiology (infection, malignancy, heart failure). For incidental nodules, ≈5% risk of malignancy depending on size (<8mm: low, >8mm: higher).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always include patient name, date, side marker, and view in your report.** Inconsistency is a major safety risk.
- **Use the "ABCDE" systematic search every time – do not just stare at the area of interest.** This catches unexpected findings (e.g., free air under diaphragm, asymmetrical hilum).
- **Correlate CXR findings with the clinical history and other imaging** (old CXRs are gold – compare to previous CT or CXR for change).
- **If you are unsure about a subtle finding, escalate to a radiologist or senior clinician with a focused question** (e.g., "Could this be a small pneumothorax?").
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT rely solely on the machine-generated report – it may miss critical details.** Always view the image yourself.
- **Do NOT ignore the quality of the film** (rotation, inspiration, penetration). A poor-quality CXR can mask pathology.
- **Do NOT label every opacification as pneumonia** – consider atelectasis, oedema, haemorrhage, effusion. Remember: air bronchogram suggests solid lung (pneumonia/oedema).
- **Do NOT order repeat CXR for a stable, previously seen small pneumothorax** – BTS guidelines recommend observation and interval imaging only if symptomatic.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness, chest pain, fever not controlled with paracetamol, haemoptysis, or inability to keep oral fluids.
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 1-2 weeks for all abnormal CXR without urgent indication. For pneumonia, repeat CXR at 4-6 weeks if age>50 or smoker to exclude underlying tumour.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any physiological parameter in red zone.
  - Tension pneumothorax (before/after decompression), massive haemothorax, or hypoxaemia despite high-flow oxygen.
  - Suspected massive PE with shock (SBP <90 mmHg).
  - Widening mediastinum >8 cm on PA CXR (aortic dissection risk).
  - CXR suggests bilateral pulmonary oedema with normal heart size – think ARDS/ALT.
- **ICU referral criteria:**
  - Need for non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) or intubation.
  - Refractory hypoxaemia (PaO2/FiO2 <200).
  - Haemodynamic instability not responding to fluid resuscitation.
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L + NEWS2 ≥5** requires immediate senior review and escalation to critical care outreach.

rawMarkdown:
## CXR — Approach
**One-liner:** A systematic method to interpret chest X-ray, prioritising life-threatening findings in acutely unwell patients.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax:** Complete white-out of hemithorax with contralateral mediastinal shift (trachea deviated away, heart borders pushed, >50% lung volume). Immediate needle decompression – do not wait for CT.
- **Massive haemothorax / large pleural effusion:** Complete or near-complete opacification of one side with mediastinal shift *away* from the opacification (differentiates from collapse). Respiratory distress + hypotension. Call chest drain team now – not after full workup.
- **Advanced bilateral pneumonia / ARDS:** Diffuse bilateral airspace opacification (white lung) with normal heart size + peripheral sparing? OR bilateral symmetrical opacities + rapidly worsening hypoxia. High PEEP, senior review, ICU referral.
- **Massive pulmonary embolism (saddle embolus):** Normal CXR but undetectable cardiac output + severe hypoxia. **Single finding:** CXR clear + clinical shock – must get CT pulmonary angiogram immediately if stable; if unstable, go straight to echocardiogram and thrombolysis.

### Presentation
- **Indications:** Dyspnoea, chest pain, cough, haemoptysis, fever, trauma, suspected pneumothorax, pleural effusion, lung cancer, pre-operative, post-line/tube check.
- **Key exam findings before CXR:** Wheeze, crackles, decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion, tracheal deviation, JVP elevation, unilateral chest expansion, hypoxia (SpO2 <92% room air).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **For opacification/consolidation:**
  - Pneumonia (often lobar, air bronchogram)
  - Atelectasis (volume loss, fissure shift)
  - Pulmonary oedema (bilateral, perihilar, cardiomegaly)
  - Haemorrhage (bilateral or focal, sudden onset)
- **For hyperlucency:**
  - Simple pneumothorax (pleural line, absent lung markings)
  - COPD/emphysema (flattened diaphragms, hyperinflation)
  - Compensatory hyperinflation after lobectomy
- **Splitter:** **Tracheal deviation + mediastinal shift** → differentiates tension (shift *away* from opacification) from massive collapse (shift *toward* opacification).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Ensure correct patient + side markers (right vs left, AP vs PA, erect vs supine).
  - **Systematic search:** ABCDE approach – Airway (trachea, carina), Breathing (lungs, pleural spaces), Cardiac (heart size, borders, mediastinal width), Diaphragm (costophrenic angles, free air under diaphragm), Everything else (bones, tubes, lines, soft tissues, gastric bubble, apical lung).
  - **Check for lines:** ET tube tip 3-5 cm above carina, CVC tip in SVC, NG tube in stomach, chest tube directed apically/posteriorly.
- **Bloods:**
  - Not directly part of CXR interpretation, but for context: FBC, CRP, U+E, blood cultures if fever, troponin if chest pain, ABG if hypoxia/respiratory failure.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** PA erect CXR (AP supine only if patient cannot sit up).
  - **Second-line:** Lateral CXR (localised opacities, effusion meniscus), CT chest (if complex effusion, suspected PE, lung cancer staging, trauma).
  - **Never-miss test:** **For unexplained hypoxia with clear CXR → CT pulmonary angiogram** (rule out PE before attributing to other causes). Also, **right-sided ECG leads** if suspecting right ventricular strain (S1Q3T3, RVH).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - *Pneumothorax size:* >2 cm measured from lung margin to chest wall at level of hilum (BTS guideline). Supine CXR – air in anterior costophrenic sulcus (deep sulcus sign).
  - *Cardiomegaly:* cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on PA film.
  - *Pleural effusion:* meniscus sign, blunting of costophrenic angle; small <400 mL.
  - *Pneumonia:* CURB-65 score (confusion, urea>7, RR≥30, BP<90/60, age≥65) used to guide admission.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Tension pneumothorax:** Needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line) → chest drain (5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line). Do not wait for CT.
  - **Massive effusion/haemothorax:** Insert large-bore chest drain (28-32Fr) – if haemothorax, consider auto-transfusion.
  - **Massive haemoptysis (opacified lung + hypovolaemia):** Lie patient on affected side to protect healthy lung, call anaesthetics/ICU for rigid bronchoscopy. Do not put in lateral position – they may choke.
  - **Suspected PE with high-probability CXR (normal):** Activate PE protocol – urgent CTPA if stable, echo if unstable.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Validate CXR findings with clinical picture. Document systematic report (use a template).
  - Repeat CXR for: new symptoms, pneumothorax after chest tube management, pneumonia not resolving after 72h, pleural effusion post-drainage.
  - Ensure oxygen therapy based on SpO2 targets (88-92% if risk of CO2 retention, else ≥94%).
  - VTE prophylaxis if prolonged immobility (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD or tinzaparin 3500 IU SC OD).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Abnormal CXR (e.g. nodule, mass, incidental finding) – refer to respiratory clinic or lung cancer MDT per local protocol.
  - Follow up resolution: e.g. repeat CXR at 4-6 weeks for pneumonia if age>50 or smoker.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 1L 0.9% NaCl over 8h if nil by mouth or vomiting – otherwise encourage oral hydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Yes, unless contraindicated (check renal function, weight, platelet count).
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM only if awaiting procedure (e.g. CT with contrast, chest drain insertion). Clear fluids ok.
  - **Observation frequency:** Baseline NEWS2; if stable on air → 4-hourly; if hypoxic/respiratory distress → 1-2 hourly until stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **No first-line drugs specific to CXR interpretation.** However, if CXR shows pneumonia, treat empirically:
  - **Community-acquired pneumonia (CURB-65 ≥2):** Doxycycline 200 mg PO/IV stat, then 100 mg OD x 7 days (if mild/moderate). Alternative: Co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS x 7 days.
  - **Hospital-acquired pneumonia (≥48h in hospital):** Seek microbiology advice; typical: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV QDS x 7 days.
  - **Acute exacerbation COPD (pneumonia excluded):** Prednisolone 30 mg PO OD x 5 days + +/- amoxicillin 500 mg PO TDS x 5 days (only if purulent sputum).
- **Key contraindications:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: allergy, avoid in penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity).
  - Doxycycline: pregnancy/lactation (relative), children <12 (tooth discolouration).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing high-concentration oxygen (e.g. 15 L/min via non-rebreather) in known COPD without target saturation monitoring (risks hypercapnic respiratory failure).** Titrate to SpO2 88-92%.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Misinterpretation leading to delayed treatment (e.g., missed tension pneumothorax, incorrectly diagnosing pneumonia in pulmonary oedema).
  - Failure to detect pneumothorax on supine CXR (deep sulcus sign or subtle hyperlucency).
  - Missed congenital or iatrogenic aortopulmonary window (mediastinal widening – could be aortic dissection).
- **Chronic:**
  - Over-investigation from incidental benign nodule – unnecessary radiation from follow-up CTs.
  - Underdiagnosis of lung cancer due to inadequate technique (poor inspiration, rotated film).

### Prognosis
- 1-3 month mortality for community-acquired pneumonia is ~10-20% in hospitalised patients; uncomplicated pneumothorax resolves in days with intervention; pleural effusion outcome depends on aetiology (infection, malignancy, heart failure). For incidental nodules, ≈5% risk of malignancy depending on size (<8mm: low, >8mm: higher).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always include patient name, date, side marker, and view in your report.** Inconsistency is a major safety risk.
- **Use the "ABCDE" systematic search every time – do not just stare at the area of interest.** This catches unexpected findings (e.g., free air under diaphragm, asymmetrical hilum).
- **Correlate CXR findings with the clinical history and other imaging** (old CXRs are gold – compare to previous CT or CXR for change).
- **If you are unsure about a subtle finding, escalate to a radiologist or senior clinician with a focused question** (e.g., "Could this be a small pneumothorax?").

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT rely solely on the machine-generated report – it may miss critical details.** Always view the image yourself.
- **Do NOT ignore the quality of the film** (rotation, inspiration, penetration). A poor-quality CXR can mask pathology.
- **Do NOT label every opacification as pneumonia** – consider atelectasis, oedema, haemorrhage, effusion. Remember: air bronchogram suggests solid lung (pneumonia/oedema).
- **Do NOT order repeat CXR for a stable, previously seen small pneumothorax** – BTS guidelines recommend observation and interval imaging only if symptomatic.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness, chest pain, fever not controlled with paracetamol, haemoptysis, or inability to keep oral fluids.
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 1-2 weeks for all abnormal CXR without urgent indication. For pneumonia, repeat CXR at 4-6 weeks if age>50 or smoker to exclude underlying tumour.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any physiological parameter in red zone.
  - Tension pneumothorax (before/after decompression), massive haemothorax, or hypoxaemia despite high-flow oxygen.
  - Suspected massive PE with shock (SBP <90 mmHg).
  - Widening mediastinum >8 cm on PA CXR (aortic dissection risk).
  - CXR suggests bilateral pulmonary oedema with normal heart size – think ARDS/ALT.
- **ICU referral criteria:**
  - Need for non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) or intubation.
  - Refractory hypoxaemia (PaO2/FiO2 <200).
  - Haemodynamic instability not responding to fluid resuscitation.
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L + NEWS2 ≥5** requires immediate senior review and escalation to critical care outreach.
